MFT.description: The $MFT is the primary metadata structure in NTFS. Every file and directory on an NTFS volume has at least one entry in the MFT. Contains $STANDARD_INFORMATION (0x10) and $FILE_NAME (0x30) attributes with independent timestamp sets, entry numbers, sequence numbers, parent references, and file attribute flags.
MFT.forensic_significance: Dual timestamp attributes ($SI vs $FN) are key to timestomping detection. $SI can be modified by user-mode APIs; $FN can only be changed by the kernel.

USNJournal.description: The $UsnJrnl ($J alternate data stream) records file system changes at a granular level including creates, deletes, renames, data modifications, and truncations. Each entry contains reason codes, timestamps, file references, and parent references.
USNJournal.forensic_significance: Provides corroborating evidence for file operations. UpdateReasons flags (FileCreate, FileDelete, DataTruncation, DataOverwrite) reveal low-level operations even when higher-level artifacts are manipulated.

Prefetch.description: Windows Prefetch files (.pf) record application execution metadata to optimize future launches. Each file tracks the executable name, run count, last 8 execution times, volume information, and files/directories accessed during startup.
Prefetch.forensic_significance: Proves application execution even after the binary is deleted. Hash in filename allows distinguishing executables run from different paths.

EVTX.description: Windows Event Logs (.evtx) record system, security, and application events. Security.evtx contains logon events (4624/4625), process creation (4688), privilege use (4672), and audit log clearing (1102). Each record has EventID, timestamp, provider, channel, and structured payload data.
EVTX.forensic_significance: Event 1102 specifically indicates audit log clearing. Cross-referencing with USN journal timestamps reveals the temporal ordering of log manipulation.

LNK.description: LNK files (.lnk) are Windows shell links that store target file metadata including creation/modification/access timestamps, file size, volume information, local/network paths, MAC address, and machine ID. LNK files preserve target timestamps from the time the shortcut was created.
LNK.forensic_significance: Preserves original target file timestamps independent of filesystem manipulation. Key artifact for detecting timestomping — LNK target creation time should match MFT $FN timestamp.

Amcache.description: Amcache.hve is a registry hive that tracks application execution and installation. Records SHA1 hashes, file paths, sizes, PE metadata (link date, binary type), publisher information, and file key timestamps for executables that have run on the system.
Amcache.forensic_significance: Provides evidence of program execution with SHA1 hash verification. Survives file deletion — the Amcache entry persists after the executable is removed.

ShimCache.description: AppCompatCache (ShimCache) is stored in the SYSTEM registry hive and records file path, last modification time, and execution flag for executables. Entries are written on system shutdown, not at execution time.
ShimCache.forensic_significance: Cache entry position indicates relative execution order. 'Executed' flag (Windows 7/8) or presence in cache (Windows 10+) indicates the file existed and was shimmed.

SRUM.description: SRUM tracks per-application resource consumption including network bytes sent/received, CPU cycle time, foreground/background context switches, memory usage, and face time. Data is stored in an ESE database (SRUDB.dat) with entries timestamped hourly.
SRUM.forensic_significance: Proves application was running at specific times with quantified resource usage. Survives clearing of Prefetch, event logs, and browser history.

UserAssist.description: UserAssist tracks GUI-based program execution via Explorer. Stored in NTUSER.DAT under Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\UserAssist. Values are ROT13 encoded. Records run count, focus count, focus time, and last execution timestamp.
UserAssist.forensic_significance: Tracks GUI program launches specifically through Explorer (Start Menu, desktop shortcuts, etc.). Complements Prefetch which tracks all execution.

ShellBags.description: ShellBags record folder access and window positioning preferences in NTUSER.DAT and UsrClass.dat. They preserve evidence of folder navigation including folders that have since been deleted, renamed, or accessed via removable media.
ShellBags.forensic_significance: Proves a user navigated to a specific folder even after the folder is deleted. Timestamps reflect actual folder access, not file modification.

JumpList.description: Jump Lists track recently and frequently accessed files per application. Stored as OLE compound files in Recent\AutomaticDestinations and CustomDestinations. Each entry contains an embedded LNK with full target metadata.
JumpList.forensic_significance: Links specific applications to specific files with timestamps and interaction counts. Machine ID and MAC address can prove which system accessed the file.

RecycleBin.description: The Recycle Bin stores deleted files with $I (metadata: original path, deletion time, file size) and $R (actual file content) pairs per user SID. Provides deletion timestamps and original file locations.
RecycleBin.forensic_significance: Records when and from where files were deleted. $I files survive even after $R (content) is purged.

BrowserHistory.description: Browser history databases record visited URLs, visit timestamps, visit counts, page titles, and transition types. Chrome uses SQLite (History file), Firefox uses places.sqlite, Edge uses Chromium-based History.
BrowserHistory.forensic_significance: Selective deletion of history entries leaves IndexedDB cache and other browser artifacts intact, creating semantic contradictions detectable via IoI rules.

OfficeMetadata.description: Office Open XML documents (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx) contain embedded metadata in docProps/core.xml including creation time, modification time, creator, and last modified by. These timestamps are independent of filesystem timestamps.
OfficeMetadata.forensic_significance: Embedded creation time predating filesystem creation time indicates timestomping — the document existed before the forged filesystem timestamp.

BAM.description: BAM tracks execution of background applications with timestamps. Stored in SYSTEM registry hive under ControlSet\Services\bam\State\UserSettings\{SID}.
BAM.forensic_significance: Provides execution timestamps for background applications. Complements Prefetch and UserAssist with different coverage.

BITS.description: BITS manages asynchronous file transfers between machines. BITS jobs can be used for persistence and data exfiltration. Job metadata includes URL, file path, creation/modification times, and transfer status.
BITS.forensic_significance: Used by APT groups for persistence (T1197). BITS jobs survive reboots and can transfer files in background without user awareness.

WMI.description: WMI provides a management infrastructure for monitoring and controlling system resources. WMI event subscriptions can be used for persistence. The WMI repository (OBJECTS.DATA) contains class definitions, instances, and event bindings.
WMI.forensic_significance: WMI event subscriptions (T1546.003) provide fileless persistence. Event filters + consumers + bindings create trigger-action pairs that survive reboots.

Sysmon.description: Sysmon is a Windows system service that logs detailed process creation (Event 1), network connections (Event 3), file creation time changes (Event 2), and other security-relevant events to Microsoft-Windows-Sysmon/Operational log.
Sysmon.forensic_significance: Event ID 2 (FileCreateTimeChange) directly detects timestomping attempts. Event ID 1 provides full command-line logging of process execution.

Thumbcache.description: Windows generates and caches thumbnail images for files viewed in Explorer. Thumbnails persist in thumbcache_*.db files even after the original files are deleted.
Thumbcache.forensic_significance: Thumbnails survive file deletion. Proves a user viewed files that no longer exist on the system.

ActivitiesCache.description: Windows Timeline tracks user activities across applications. Stored in ActivitiesCache.db SQLite database. Records application usage, file access, and web browsing with timestamps and duration.
ActivitiesCache.forensic_significance: Cross-device activity tracking. Records application focus time and content payloads that survive clearing of browser history and recent files.

VSS.description: VSS creates point-in-time snapshots of volumes. Shadow copies are stored under System Volume Information and contain GUID-identified snapshot directories. Deletion of shadow copies removes recovery points but leaves infrastructure artifacts.
VSS.forensic_significance: VSS infrastructure files persist after shadow copy deletion. Absence of GUID directories when infrastructure exists indicates deliberate deletion (AF-004 IoI pattern).

WindowsRegistry.description: The Windows Registry is a hierarchical database storing OS and application configuration. Key hives: SAM (user accounts), SECURITY (security policies), SOFTWARE (installed software), SYSTEM (hardware/services config), NTUSER.DAT (per-user settings), UsrClass.dat (per-user shell settings).
WindowsRegistry.forensic_significance: Central configuration store. MRU lists, USB history, network connections, installed software, user activity, and persistence mechanisms all stored in registry.

OpenSaveMRU.description: Records files opened or saved via Windows common dialog boxes. Tracks file extensions and full paths of recently accessed files per user.
OpenSaveMRU.forensic_significance: Proves user interacted with specific files through Open/Save dialogs, even if files are deleted.

MRULocations.description: Various registry locations that track recently accessed files, folders, commands, and typed URLs. Includes RecentDocs, RunMRU, TypedPaths, TypedURLs, and application-specific MRU lists.
MRULocations.forensic_significance: Comprehensive user activity tracking across multiple interaction types.

SCCM.description: SCCM/MECM tracks software inventory, deployment history, and execution logs. The RecentlyUsedApps WMI class logs application usage with timestamps.
SCCM.forensic_significance: Enterprise-managed systems retain execution history through SCCM even when local artifacts are cleared.

Superfetch.description: SuperFetch (renamed SysMain in Windows 10) preloads frequently used applications. AgAppLaunch.db tracks application launch history with timestamps.
Superfetch.forensic_significance: Provides execution evidence independent of Prefetch .pf files.

RecentFileCache.description: Windows 7 artifact that records recently executed files. Replaced by Amcache in Windows 8+. Contains file paths of executed binaries.
RecentFileCache.forensic_significance: Windows 7 execution artifact. Simple file path list without timestamps.

RDPBitmapCache.description: Remote Desktop Protocol caches screen fragments as bitmap tiles in bcache*.bmc files. These tiles can be reassembled to reconstruct what the user saw during RDP sessions.
RDPBitmapCache.forensic_significance: Visual evidence of remote sessions even after connection logs are cleared.

WindowsFileHistory.description: Windows File History backs up user files periodically. The Configuration directory contains EDB catalogs tracking backed-up files, paths, and timestamps.
WindowsFileHistory.forensic_significance: Prior versions of files may exist in File History even after deletion or modification.

WindowsSearch.description: Windows Search indexes file content and metadata in an ESE database. Contains file paths, timestamps, content snippets, email subjects, and other searchable properties.
WindowsSearch.forensic_significance: Contains indexed content of deleted files. Email content, document text, and metadata survive file deletion.

NetworkConnections.description: Windows tracks network connections through multiple artifacts: WLAN AutoConfig logs, NetworkList registry keys, SRUM network data, and TCP/IP parameters.
NetworkConnections.forensic_significance: Network profile history reveals geographic and temporal connection patterns.

BitLocker.description: BitLocker encrypts entire volumes using AES. Recovery keys, encryption metadata, and TPM state are tracked in registry and BCD store.
BitLocker.location: BCD store; SYSTEM registry; Recovery key file
BitLocker.forensic_significance: Encrypted volumes require recovery keys for forensic access. Key protector metadata reveals encryption timeline.

EFS.description: EFS encrypts individual files and folders using per-file encryption keys (FEK) wrapped with user certificates. EFS metadata is stored in NTFS alternate data streams.
EFS.forensic_significance: EFS-encrypted files require user certificate or recovery agent for decryption.

CCleaner.description: CCleaner is a system cleaning tool that removes temporary files, browser history, cookies, and other artifacts. Its own log and INI file record what was cleaned and when.
CCleaner.forensic_significance: CCleaner's own artifacts (MRU, ini settings) reveal what was targeted for deletion and when.

ScheduledTasks.description: Scheduled Tasks are stored as XML files defining trigger conditions, actions, and execution history. Task Scheduler operational log records execution timestamps.
ScheduledTasks.forensic_significance: Persistence mechanism (T1053). Scheduled tasks survive reboots and can execute arbitrary commands.

ETW.description: ETW is a kernel-level tracing framework. ETL trace files contain detailed provider-specific events. Used by Windows Defender, .NET runtime, networking stack, and many system components.
ETW.forensic_significance: Low-level kernel events often survive clearing of standard event logs.

SetupAPILogs.description: Setup API logs record device installation and driver loading events. setupapi.dev.log tracks USB and other device connections with timestamps.
SetupAPILogs.forensic_significance: First-connection timestamps for USB devices. Survives registry clearing.

PowerShellLogs.description: PowerShell generates multiple log sources: ScriptBlock logging (Event 4104), Module logging, Transcription logs, and PSReadLine ConsoleHost_history.txt. Each captures different aspects of PowerShell execution.
PowerShellLogs.forensic_significance: Full command history and script content. PSReadLine history persists across sessions and survives event log clearing.

CLFS.description: CLFS is a kernel logging subsystem used by Windows for transactional operations. BLF files contain log metadata; container files hold actual log records. Used by TxF/TxR transactional NTFS.
CLFS.forensic_significance: CLFS vulnerabilities (CVE-2023-23376 etc.) are actively exploited. Log records can reveal transactional operations.

LinuxLogs.description: Linux logs include auth.log (authentication events), syslog (system events), kern.log (kernel messages), and systemd journal. Log rotation preserves historical entries.
LinuxLogs.forensic_significance: Authentication attempts, sudo usage, service starts/stops, kernel events. auth.log is critical for login tracking.

BashHistory.description: Shell history files record commands executed by users. .bash_history, .zsh_history, .sh_history preserve command sequences. Some shells support timestamps (HISTTIMEFORMAT).
BashHistory.forensic_significance: Direct evidence of commands executed by a user. May include passwords, IP addresses, and file operations.

Crontab.description: Cron jobs schedule recurring tasks. User crontabs, system crontabs, and cron.d entries define scheduled commands with timing specifications.
Crontab.forensic_significance: Persistence mechanism on Linux/macOS. Adversaries use cron for recurring execution (T1053.003).

ExtFilesystem.description: The ext family of filesystems is the default for most Linux distributions. ext4 supports journaling, extents, and nanosecond timestamps. Inode metadata includes creation time (crtime) in ext4.
ExtFilesystem.forensic_significance: ext4 crtime (birth time) is immutable, analogous to NTFS $FN timestamps for timestomping detection.

MacOSFSEvents.description: FSEvents logs record file system changes at the volume level. Stored as gzip-compressed binary files, they track file/folder creation, modification, deletion, and permission changes.
MacOSFSEvents.forensic_significance: Comprehensive filesystem change log. Events persist across reboots and are difficult to selectively manipulate.

MacOSUnifiedLogs.description: Apple's unified logging system (os_log) captures system, application, and user events. Stored in tracev3 format with structured predicates and subsystem categorization.
MacOSUnifiedLogs.forensic_significance: Extremely detailed system activity log. Process execution, network activity, and user actions all recorded.

MacOSPlist.description: Plist files store configuration and state data in XML or binary format. LaunchAgents/LaunchDaemons define persistence. Application preferences, recent items, and system configuration stored as plists.
MacOSPlist.forensic_significance: LaunchAgents/LaunchDaemons are primary macOS persistence mechanism (T1543.001). Application preferences reveal usage patterns.

MacOSQuarantine.description: macOS tracks downloaded files via the com.apple.quarantine extended attribute and the QuarantineEventsV2 SQLite database. Records download URL, timestamp, and downloading application.
MacOSQuarantine.forensic_significance: Proves file download history including source URL and downloading application, even after file deletion.

MacOSKnowledgeC.description: KnowledgeC.db tracks user activity including application usage, device state, media playback, and Safari browsing. Apple's Siri Suggestions and Screen Time use this data.
MacOSKnowledgeC.forensic_significance: Comprehensive user activity timeline. Application focus time, device lock/unlock, and media interactions.

SpotlightDB.description: Spotlight indexes file content and metadata for search. The store.db and .store.db files contain indexed file paths, content types, modification dates, and content snippets.
SpotlightDB.forensic_significance: Contains metadata of deleted files. Indexed content persists after file removal.

iOSKeychain.description: iOS Keychain stores passwords, certificates, encryption keys, and secure notes in an encrypted SQLite database. Protected by hardware-bound keys and user passcode.
iOSKeychain.location: keychain-2.db (in iOS backup or filesystem extraction)
iOSKeychain.forensic_significance: Stored passwords, WiFi credentials, and app tokens. Requires device passcode or BFU/AFU extraction.

AndroidSQLite.description: Android apps store data in SQLite databases. Key databases include contacts2.db, mmssms.db, calllog.db, and app-specific databases for WhatsApp, Signal, Telegram, etc.
AndroidSQLite.forensic_significance: Direct access to application data including messages, contacts, call history, and location data.

SIMCard.description: SIM cards store subscriber identity (IMSI), phone numbers (MSISDN), SMS messages, contacts (ADN), last dialed numbers (LND), and network authentication data.
SIMCard.forensic_significance: Subscriber identity, deleted SMS, and contact records. SIM data may differ from phone data indicating SIM swapping.

PCAP.description: PCAP files contain raw network packet data captured from network interfaces. PCAPNG extends the format with interface metadata, name resolution, and comment blocks.
PCAP.location: Captured via tcpdump, Wireshark, or network TAPs
PCAP.forensic_significance: Full network conversation reconstruction. DNS queries, HTTP requests, file transfers, and credential exposure.

ZeekLogs.description: Zeek generates structured logs from network traffic: conn.log (connections), dns.log (queries), http.log (requests), files.log (transferred files), ssl.log (TLS sessions), and many more.
ZeekLogs.forensic_significance: High-level connection summaries without full packet capture overhead. C2 detection, data exfiltration measurement, DNS tunneling identification.

MemoryDump.description: RAM dumps capture the entire contents of physical memory including running processes, network connections, loaded DLLs, open file handles, registry hives, encryption keys, and user credentials.
MemoryDump.location: Captured via WinPMEM, FTK Imager, DumpIt, or LiME
MemoryDump.forensic_significance: Captures volatile state lost at shutdown. Running malware, injected code, decrypted data, and network connections only available in memory.

Hiberfil.description: hiberfil.sys contains a compressed memory dump from the last hibernation. Contains the same forensic artifacts as a live memory dump but persists on disk.
Hiberfil.forensic_significance: Memory snapshot persisted to disk. May contain credentials, decrypted data, and process state from before hibernation.

ESEDatabase.description: ESE (also known as JET Blue) is a database engine used by Windows components. SRUM (SRUDB.dat), Windows Search (Windows.edb), Exchange, and Active Directory all use ESE format.
ESEDatabase.forensic_significance: Container format for multiple forensic artifacts. Understanding ESE structure enables parsing of SRUM, Windows Search, and other ESE-backed stores.

SQLiteDB.description: SQLite is an embedded database used by browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari), messaging apps, iOS/Android system databases, and many applications. WAL (Write-Ahead Log) and journal files may contain deleted records.
SQLiteDB.forensic_significance: WAL and journal files contain records that survived deletion from the main database. Freelist pages may hold recoverable data.

OLECompound.description: OLE Compound Binary File format is used by legacy Office documents (.doc, .xls, .ppt), Thumbs.db, and Jump Lists. Contains a mini filesystem with streams and storage objects.
OLECompound.forensic_significance: Legacy document metadata, embedded objects, and macros. Jump Lists use OLE format for automatic destinations.

EXIF.description: EXIF metadata embedded in JPEG, TIFF, and RAW image files. Contains camera make/model, GPS coordinates, timestamps, exposure settings, and thumbnail images.
EXIF.forensic_significance: Geolocation, device identification, and timestamp verification. GPS coordinates place the camera at a specific location.
